1: The fourth year of Jehoiakim (605 BCE) is the year of the battle of Carchemish, when Nebuchadnezzar defeated Egypt and became the dominant power in the ancient Near East — the decisive geopolitical turning point that validates Jeremiah's northern-foe oracles. The command to write comes at this moment of historical confirmation.
2: qach lecha megillat sefer vechatavta eleiha et kol-hadvarim asher dibarti eleicha al-Yisrael ve-al-Yehudah ve-al-kol-hagoyim miyom dibbarti eleicha miyemei Yoshiyahu ve-ad hayom hazeh: 'Take a scroll and write in it all the words I have spoken to you against Israel, against Judah, and against all the nations, from the day I first spoke to you in the days of Josiah until this day.' Megillat sefer (scroll of a book) — the Hebrew writing medium of the period. The command to write is unprecedented in Jeremiah's ministry up to this point: twenty-plus years of prophetic activity are now to be committed to permanent record. The scroll will outlast its oral delivery and reach those who were not present to hear. This chapter is self-referentially about the nature and indestructibility of the written word.
3: ulai yishme'u beit Yehudah et kol-hara-ah asher anochi choshev la-asot lahem lema-an yashuvu ish midarcho hara-ah: 'Perhaps the house of Judah will hear of all the disaster I intend to bring on them, so that each person will turn from his evil way, and I will forgive their iniquity and their sin.' The divine motive for writing: restoration through repentance remains the goal. Even at this late stage — the battle of Carchemish has confirmed the worst — YHWH leaves open the possibility of repentance and forgiveness. Ulai (perhaps) is not divine uncertainty but prophetic openness: the door is not closed until it is demonstrably closed.
4: vayiqra Yirmiyahu et-Baruch ben-Neriyah vayichtov Baruch mipi Yirmiyahu et kol-divrei YHWH asher diber elav al-megillat sefer: 'So Jeremiah summoned Baruch son of Neriah, and Baruch wrote on a scroll everything Jeremiah dictated to him — all the words of the LORD that had been spoken to Jeremiah.' Baruch son of Neriah is Jeremiah's scribe and the likely primary editor of the book of Jeremiah. A seal (bulla) bearing the inscription 'Belonging to Berekhyahu son of Neriyahu the scribe' was found among the Moussaieff collection, dated to the sixth century BCE — an extraordinary extra-biblical confirmation of Baruch's historical existence and professional title.
5: vaytzav Yirmiyahu et-Baruch lemor ani atzur lo uchal lavo beit YHWH: 'Jeremiah gave Baruch these instructions: I am banned; I cannot go to the house of the LORD.' Atzur (banned/confined/prevented) — the cause of Jeremiah's ban is not specified here, but may be related to his temple sermon (ch. 7, 26) or earlier controversies. His inability to deliver the scroll personally necessitates Baruch's role as his agent — a role Baruch takes on with apparent courage, since reading the scroll publicly would carry risk.
6: uvata viqrata bamegillah asher katavta mipi et-divrei YHWH be-oznei ha-am beit YHWH beyom tzom: 'So you go and read from the scroll ... to all the people in the LORD's house on a day of fasting.' The fast-day setting maximizes the audience: pilgrims from throughout Judah (kol-anshei Yehudah haba-im me-areihem) would be present for a communal fast. The public reading on a solemn occasion — when people are most psychologically prepared to hear weighty words — is the optimal context for the scroll's reception.
7: ulai tipol techintam lifnei YHWH veyashuvu ish midarcho hara-ah: The same ulai (perhaps) of hope from v. 3 — the possibility of repentance remains. The petition (techinnatam) Jeremiah hopes the people will bring before YHWH echoes the temple dedication prayer structure (1 Kgs 8:46-53). The two-fold motivation — the 'great anger and wrath' pronounced and the open door of forgiveness — frame the reading as both warning and invitation.
8: Baruch's obedience to Jeremiah's command: vayya'as Baruch ben-Neriyahu kekol asher tzivahu Yirmiyahu hanavi (Baruch son of Neriah did exactly what the prophet Jeremiah commanded him). The precision mirrors the Rechabites' obedience to their ancestor — but the command comes from a prophet, not merely a human ancestor.
9: The fifth year of Jehoiakim, ninth month (approximately December 604 BCE) — a specific calendar date. A communal fast was proclaimed, possibly in response to news of the Babylonian threat after Carchemish. The fast is an act of communal penitence or petition before YHWH — precisely the context for the scroll's message to be heard. The crowds from the towns of Judah (kol-ha-am mi-arei Yehudah) expand the reach beyond Jerusalem's population.
10: vayiqra Baruch bamegilah et-divrei Yirmiyahu beit YHWH belishkat Gemariyahu ben-Shaphan hasopher bechatzer ha-elyon: 'Then Baruch read the words of Jeremiah from the scroll in the house of the LORD, in the chamber of Gemariah son of Shaphan the secretary.' Gemariah son of Shaphan connects to the powerful Shaphan family that supported Jeremiah throughout his ministry (2 Kgs 22:3-14: Shaphan the secretary read the law-scroll to Josiah; Ahikam son of Shaphan protected Jeremiah in ch. 26). The chamber's location at the upper court, at the New Gate, gave maximum public access — the scroll was read in a major civic and religious space.
11: Micaiah son of Gemariah — Shaphan's grandson — hears the scroll and immediately takes the content to the court officials. The chain of transmission (scroll → Baruch → upper court → Micaiah → officials' chamber) traces the word's movement from prophetic origin to political center. Each link is named, establishing accountability and historical memory.
12: The officials listed — Elishama the secretary, Delaiah son of Shemaiah, Elnathan son of Achbor (who had helped retrieve the prophet Uriah from Egypt, 26:22), Gemariah son of Shaphan, Zedekiah son of Hananiah — represent the upper tier of Jehoiakim's court. Their response (v. 16: they were afraid) differs markedly from the king's (v. 23-24: he showed no fear).
13: The reporting of Micaiah to the officials: the scroll's content is relayed through successive audiences — each hearing generating a response that shapes the next audience's encounter with the word. The word's movement through institutions is itself part of its narrative: it finds its way despite the obstacles.
14: The officials send for Baruch directly: qach et-hamegillah asher qarata bah be-oznei ha-am ule-ch (take the scroll from which you read to the people and come). Their summons uses the scroll as its subject — the scroll has its own identity as the locus of the word. Baruch comes with the scroll to the officials' chamber.
15: The officials' first response — 'Sit down and read it to us' (shev na veqra-ennu be-ozeinu) — is one of respectful attention. They are alarmed but not hostile (v. 16). The contrast with Jehoiakim's response (vv. 23-24) is the pivot of the chapter: the officials respond with fear; the king responds with contempt.
16: vayehi kishomam et kol-hadvarim pahedu ish el-re-ehu vayomru el-Baruch haged nagid lamelech et kol-hadvarim ha-elleh: 'When they heard all the words, they turned to one another in alarm and said to Baruch: We must report all these words to the king.' Pachadu (alarm/fear) — the appropriate prophetic response to the divine word (cf. Isa 66:2, 5: 'this is the one I esteem: he who is humble and contrite in spirit, and trembles at my word'). The officials hear the word and are afraid — the fear is right; the failure is that they report to the king rather than leading repentance from their position.
17: The officials' investigative question — how did Baruch write all these words? (at Jeremiah's dictation?) — establishes the chain of authority for legal purposes: is this Baruch's composition or Jeremiah's, and is it the prophet's own word or YHWH's? The question also protects Baruch legally: it creates a record that he was acting as a scribe, not as a prophet making his own pronouncements.
18: vayomer lahem Baruch mipi yiqra elai et kol-hadvarim ha-elleh va-ani kotev al-hasefer bedyo: 'Baruch answered them: He dictated all these words to me, and I wrote them in ink on the scroll.' Bedyo (in ink) — a technical scribal note. The specificity of 'ink on a scroll' underscores the permanence of the written word compared to the spoken oracle: this is now fixed in a physical medium that can be preserved, copied, and transmitted beyond the immediate audience.
19: vayomru hasarim el-Baruch lek histater atah viYirmiyahu ve-ish al-yeda ei-zeh atem: 'Then the officials said to Baruch: Go and hide — you and Jeremiah — and let no one know where you are.' The officials are protecting Jeremiah and Baruch from the king's anticipated rage. Their warning implies knowledge of Jehoiakim's character — they know what he will do when he hears the scroll. The intervention parallels earlier protection: Ahikam son of Shaphan protected Jeremiah in 26:24.
20: The scroll is stored in Elishama the secretary's chamber while the officials brief the king. The act of storage is significant: even before the king hears its contents, the scroll is being treated as a document requiring custody — both for preservation and as evidence. The officials apparently try to maintain the scroll's existence even while reporting its contents.
21: vayishlach hamelech et-Yehudi laqachat et-hamegillah: 'The king sent Jehudi to get the scroll.' The king sends a servant to retrieve the physical object — Jehoiakim wants the scroll itself, not just a report of its contents. The retrieval of the scroll from Elishama's chamber establishes that what follows is a deliberate, calculated act against the word itself, not an impulsive moment.
22: vehamelech yoshev beit hachore bachodesh hateshii ve-et haach burneth lefanav: 'The king was sitting in the winter apartment in the ninth month, and there was a fire burning before him in the fire pot.' The domestic detail — the king in his winter quarters, warming himself by the fire — creates the ironic tableau: the fire that warms the king will consume the word. The same fire that Jehoiakim uses for comfort will become the instrument of his act of defiance.
23: vayehi ki qara Yehudi shalosh daltot ve-arba-ah yiqra-ennu vayikra-enhu hamelech bitar lapid hasopher vehishlikh el-ha-esh asher el-haach ad tom kol-hamegillah al-ha-esh (wayhî kî-qārāʾ yĕhûdî šāloš dĕlātôt wĕʾarbaʿāh yiqrāʾennû wĕyiqrāʿehu hammelek bĕtaʿar hassōpēr wĕhišlîk ʾel-hāʾēš ʾăšer ʾel-hāʾaḥ ʿad tōm kol-hamĕgillāh ʿal-hāʾēš): 'Each time Jehudi had read three or four columns, the king would cut the section off with a scribe's knife and throw it into the fire, until the entire scroll had been consumed.' Taar hasopher (scribe's knife, penknife) — the tool used for cutting reed pens and trimming scrolls, now used for defacement. The deliberate, methodical destruction — column by column, not in a single outburst — makes the act calculated rather than impulsive: Jehoiakim is consciously and continuously destroying the word of God. The contrast with Josiah's response when he heard the law read (qara et-bigadav, he tore his garments — 2 Kgs 22:11) is complete: one king tears himself; the other tears the scroll.
24: velo pakhadu velo qar-u et-bigdeihem hamelech vekhol-avadav hashom-im et kol-hadvarim ha-elleh: 'Yet neither the king nor any of his servants who heard all these words was afraid, nor did they tear their garments.' Lo pachadu velo qaru et-bigdeihem (neither afraid nor did they tear their garments) — the double absence of appropriate response. Fear and garment-tearing were the two marks of authentic covenant response to YHWH's word (2 Kgs 22:11, 19: Josiah tore his garments; Hezekiah sent messengers with torn garments to Isaiah in Isa 37:1). The king and his servants have hardened hearts that cannot receive the word — the Pharaoh-analogy is implicit: Exod 7:13, 22 ('Pharaoh's heart was hardened') uses the same vocabulary of stubborn non-response.
25: vegam Elnathan veDel-ayahu veGamarayahu pichalelu ba-melech levilti srifal et-hamegillah velo shama aleichem: 'Even though Elnathan and Delaiah and Gemariah pleaded with the king not to burn the scroll, he would not listen to them.' Three officials (including Gemariah son of Shaphan, who had provided the chamber for the public reading) interceded on behalf of the scroll. Their pleading (hiphgilu) is the same word used for prophetic intercession — they are using prophetic-style advocacy for the preservation of the prophetic word. The king's refusal to listen to them mirrors his refusal to listen to the scroll.
26: vaytzav hamelech et-Yerachmeel ben-hamelech ve-et-Seraya ben-Azriel ve-et-Shelemyahu ben-Avdeel laqachat et-Baruch hasopher ve-et-Yirmiyahu hanavi vayastirem YHWH: 'The king commanded Jerahmeel a royal prince, Seraiah son of Azriel, and Shelemiah son of Abdeel to seize Baruch the scribe and Jeremiah the prophet. But the LORD hid them.' Vayastirem YHWH (but the LORD hid them) — the divine protection intervenes at the moment of royal persecution. The brevity of the clause — four Hebrew words — is its strength: no explanation of how, simply the fact that YHWH protected what Jehoiakim sought to destroy. The same word sitr (hide) appears in 33:3's 'hidden things' (betzurot) — but now it is not revelation that is hidden but the prophet himself, preserved by divine concealment.
27: After the scroll's destruction, the word of YHWH comes to Jeremiah — the divine response to the burning. The indestructibility of the word is the oracle's premise: burning the scroll does not destroy the word; it only generates a second, fuller scroll.
28: shuv qach lecha megillah acheret vechtovchetah aleha et kol-hadvarim harishonim asher hayu al-hamegillah harishonah asher saraf Yehoyaqim melech Yehudah ba-esh: 'Take another scroll and write on it all the same words that were in the first scroll, the one Jehoiakim king of Judah burned.' The command to replace the destroyed scroll is the theological statement: the word of YHWH cannot be annihilated by human action. This is the OT enactment of what Jesus will claim: 'heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will never pass away' (Matt 24:35; Mark 13:31; Luke 21:33). The word outlasts the political power that seeks to silence it.
29: ve-al Yehoyaqim melech Yehudah tomar koh-amar YHWH atah sarafta et-hamegillah hazot lemor madu katavtah aleiha lemor bo yavo melech Bavel veshichet et-ha-aretz hazot: 'And concerning Jehoiakim king of Judah you shall say: Thus says the LORD — You burned this scroll, asking: Why have you written in it that the king of Babylon will certainly come and destroy this land?' The oracle is addressed directly to Jehoiakim's act of burning — YHWH names the specific question behind the destruction: Jehoiakim burned the scroll because he rejected the Babylonian-threat oracle it contained. The burning is an act of wishful thinking — destroy the word and the future it announces will not come. The divine response makes the burning ineffective and the burning-king the new subject of prophecy.
30: lachen koh-amar YHWH al-Yehoyaqim melech Yehudah lo-yihyeh lo yoshev al-kise David venivlatoh tihyeh mushleket lechoreh yomam velaqarachah laylah: 'Therefore thus says the LORD concerning Jehoiakim king of Judah: He will have no one to sit on the throne of David, and his dead body will be cast out to the heat of the day and the frost of the night.' The throne-denial echoes the Coniah curse of 22:30 ('none of his descendants will succeed in sitting on the throne of David') — consistent: Jehoiakim's son Jehoiachin reigned only three months before being deported; neither he nor any descendant ever ruled Judah as an independent king. The exposed body (mushleket) mirrors the 'buried like a donkey' oracle of 22:19.
31: ufaqadeti alav ve-al-zaro ve-al-avadav et-avonam: 'I will punish him and his offspring and his servants for their iniquity.' The paqad avon (punish iniquity / visit iniquity) formula — the covenant curse-enactment. The household solidarity of punishment (king + offspring + servants) is the corporate consequence of covenantal violation. The repeated phrase 'because they would not listen' (ya-an asher lo shamu) makes refusal to hear the single sufficient explanation for everything that follows.
32: viYirmiyahu laqach megillah acheret vayitenah el-Baruch ben-Neriyahu hasopher vayichtov aleiha mipi Yirmiyahu et kol-divrei hasefer asher saraf Yehoyaqim melech Yehudah ba-esh vaiyosef aleichem od devarim rabbim kehamah: 'Then Jeremiah took another scroll and gave it to Baruch son of Neriah the scribe, who wrote on it at Jeremiah's dictation all the words of the scroll that Jehoiakim king of Judah had burned. And many similar words were added to them.' The burned scroll is replaced — and exceeded. Vayosef aleichem od devarim rabbim kehamah (many similar words were added to them): the attempt to silence the word results in more words than before. The pattern of attempted suppression leading to amplification of the word is the dynamic of the entire canon: persecution does not diminish the word but occasions its expansion. The book of Jeremiah itself — 52 chapters — is the final ironic monument to Jehoiakim's burning of the first scroll.
